event: Paleo-Indian hunter-gatherers settle the region of present-day Honduras, leaving stone tools and rock art such as those near La Esperanza.
Early agricultural communities develop, cultivating maize, beans, and squash and establishing settled village life.
Maya-influenced cultures emerge in western Honduras, with growing trade networks linking the region to Mesoamerica.
K'inich Yax K'uk' Mo' founds the Maya royal dynasty at Copán, beginning the city's rise as a major Classic Maya center.
Copán's ruler Uaxaclajuun Ubaah K'awiil (18 Rabbit) is captured and beheaded by the rival city of Quiriguá.
Copán's last known monument is dedicated as the Maya city enters decline and is gradually abandoned.
Christopher Columbus reaches the Bay Islands and the Caribbean coast on his fourth voyage, naming the cape Gracias a Dios.
Spanish conquistadors led by Gil González Dávila and Cristóbal de Olid begin the conquest of Honduras.
The Lenca leader Lempira leads a major indigenous revolt against Spanish rule before being killed, becoming a national symbol of resistance.
The city of Comayagua is established and later becomes the colonial capital of the province of Honduras.
Rich silver deposits are discovered near Tegucigalpa, spurring a mining boom and the growth of the settlement.
English and Dutch pirates sack and destroy the port of Trujillo, reflecting European rivalry over the Caribbean coast.
The British deport thousands of Garifuna from St. Vincent to the island of Roatán, founding the Garifuna communities of Honduras's coast.
Honduras gains independence from Spain on 15 September as part of the Central American declaration of independence.
Honduras joins the United Provinces of Central America, a federal republic of the former Spanish provinces.
Honduras declares itself an independent sovereign state following the dissolution of the Federal Republic of Central America.
American filibuster William Walker is captured and executed at Trujillo after attempting to seize control in Central America.
Tegucigalpa is designated the permanent national capital, replacing Comayagua.
U.S. fruit companies begin large-scale banana plantation operations on the north coast, launching the country's banana export economy.
Civil conflict tied to U.S. business interests deepens the country's reputation as a 'banana republic'.
The United Fruit Company absorbs Cuyamel, consolidating overwhelming foreign control over Honduras's banana industry.
Tiburcio Carías Andino becomes president and establishes a long authoritarian dictatorship lasting until 1949.
Honduras becomes a founding member of the United Nations.
A massive strike against the banana companies wins recognition of labor rights and spurs the growth of the Honduran labor movement.
Ramón Villeda Morales is elected president and launches social and land reforms during a period of civilian rule.
Colonel Oswaldo López Arellano seizes power in a military coup, beginning decades of frequent army dominance over politics.
The brief 'Football War' erupts with El Salvador over migration and border tensions, leaving thousands dead before a ceasefire.
Hurricane Fifi devastates the north coast, killing thousands and causing catastrophic damage to crops and infrastructure.
Honduras and El Salvador sign a peace treaty formally ending hostilities from the 1969 war.
A new constitution takes effect and civilian rule returns with the inauguration of President Roberto Suazo Córdova.
Honduras becomes a base for U.S.-backed Contra forces fighting Nicaragua's Sandinista government, militarizing the country during the Cold War.
The International Court of Justice settles the long-standing land and maritime border dispute with El Salvador.
Hurricane Mitch strikes with catastrophic force, killing thousands and destroying much of the country's infrastructure and agriculture.
Honduras joins the Central America-Dominican Republic Free Trade Agreement (CAFTA-DR) with the United States.
President Manuel Zelaya is removed in a military-backed coup and expelled from the country, triggering an international crisis.
Porfirio Lobo takes office after contested elections held in the aftermath of the coup.
Honduras records one of the world's highest homicide rates, driven by drug trafficking and gang violence, prompting major emigration.
A surge of unaccompanied Honduran children and families migrating to the United States highlights the country's violence and poverty crisis.
President Juan Orlando Hernández is declared winner of a disputed reelection, sparking protests and accusations of fraud.
Large 'migrant caravans' of Hondurans set out for the United States, drawing global attention to regional migration pressures.
Back-to-back hurricanes Eta and Iota strike within weeks, causing widespread flooding, destruction, and displacement.
Xiomara Castro is inaugurated as the first woman president of Honduras.
Former president Juan Orlando Hernández is convicted in a U.S. court on drug trafficking and weapons charges and sentenced to a long prison term.
